saida_bruta:
“You look familiar,” he said courteously. “Weren’t you in the First Division during the war?”

“Yes, I served with the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I was in the Sixteenth until June of ’18. That’s why you seemed familiar.”

We reminisced briefly about some dreary, rain-soaked villages in France. Apparently, he lived nearby, since he mentioned having just bought a hydroplane and planned to test it the next morning.

“Would you like to come along, old sport? We’ll just fly close to the shore over the Sound.”

“What time?”

“Whenever you like.”

I was about to ask his name when Jordan glanced over and smiled.

“Enjoying yourself?” she asked.

“Much more now.” I turned back to the man beside me. “This sort of party isn’t really my scene. I haven’t even met the host—I live just over there.” I gestured toward the distant, unseen hedge. “Mr. Gatsby sent his chauffeur with an invitation.”

He looked at me for a moment as if confused.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said quietly.

“Oh!” I blurted. “I’m terribly sorry.”

“I assumed you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not much of a host.”

“You look familiar,” he said with a friendly smile. “Were you in the First Division during the war?”

“Yes, I served with the Twenty-Eighth Infantry.”

“I was with the Sixteenth until June of ’18. That must be why you seem familiar.”

We reminisced about dreary, rain-soaked villages in France. He mentioned that he lived nearby and had just bought a hydroplane, which he intended to test the next morning.

“Would you like to come along? We could fly up the coast over the Sound.”

“What time should we leave?”

“Anytime you like.”

Just as I was about to ask his name, Jordan glanced over and smiled.

“Are you enjoying yourself?” she asked.

“Much more now.” I turned to him. “Honestly, I’m not much for gatherings like this. I haven’t even met the host—I live just over there.” I nodded toward the distant hedge. “Mr. Gatsby’s chauffeur brought me an invitation.”

He hesitated, studying me with a faint look of confusion.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said quietly.

“Oh!” I blurted, taken aback. “I’m terribly sorry.”

“I assumed you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not much of a host.”

“Much more now,” I replied. Then, turning to him, I added, “To be honest, I don’t care much for these parties. I haven’t even met the host—I live just over there.” I nodded toward the distant hedge. “Mr. Gatsby’s chauffeur brought me the invitation.”

He hesitated, looking slightly confused.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said quietly.

“Oh!” I exclaimed, surprised. “I’m terribly sorry.”

“I assumed you knew, old sport. I’m not much of a host, I’m afraid.

saida_bruta:
“You look familiar,” he said, giving me a warm smile. “Were you in the First Division during the war?”

“Yes, I served with the Twenty-Eighth Infantry.”

“I was with the Sixteenth until June of ’18. That’s probably why I seem familiar.”

We spent a few moments recalling the gray, rain-soaked villages of France. He mentioned he lived nearby and had just purchased a hydroplane, which he planned to test the following morning.

“Would you care to join me? We could fly up the coast, over the Sound.”

“What time should we meet?”

“Whenever you’re available.”

Before I could ask his name, Jordan glanced over and smiled.

“Are you having a good time?” she asked.

“Much more now,” I replied. Turning to him, I added, “Honestly, I’m not much for these gatherings. I haven’t even met the host—I live just over there.” I gestured toward the distant hedge. “Mr. Gatsby’s chauffeur delivered my invitation.”

He hesitated, looking mildly perplexed.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said quietly.

“Oh!” I blurted out, surprised. “I’m terribly sorry.”

“I thought you knew, old sport. I’m not much of a host, I’m afraid.”
